tasks.name: Draft Deliberate Indifference Claim (Count I)
tasks.description: Count I: Title IX deliberate indifference. Tests exhibit selection for knowledge, indifference, severity, and access denial elements.
tasks.parties_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendant Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit is a private university receiving federal financial assistance, located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

Individual actors referenced in the record include:
- Dr. Adam Wasilko, Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students
- Alicia Simpson, Title IX Coordinator and Director of Sexual Misconduct Prevention and Response
- Dr. Anne Sawa, Executive Director of Student Conduct
- Dr. Daniel Selcer, Associate Professor and Chair, Department of Philosophy
- Dr. Kristine Blair, Dean, McAnulty College of Liberal Arts
tasks.defendant_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendant Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit is a private university receiving federal financial assistance, located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
tasks.required_elements: (1) Actual knowledge by official with authority to act; (2) Clearly unreasonable response (deliberate indifference); (3) Severe, pervasive, objectively offensive harassment; (4) Denial of equal educational access
tasks.sumf:
1. Plaintiff David Mullins reported harassment by a fellow graduate student to Dean Wasilko on June 30, 2025, describing a physical pursuit and confrontation, and requesting emergency housing assistance. (Exhibit B-0.)

2. On July 24, 2025, Plaintiff sent a formal Title IX escalation to Simpson, the university president, provost, and legal affairs, citing 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a) and demanding supportive measures within 48 hours. (SAC ¶¶ 168-172.)

3. On August 13, 2025, Plaintiff sent an Emergency Removal Request to Title IX Coordinator Simpson, citing § 106.44(c), attaching sworn declarations and a physician's letter documenting safety concerns. (Exhibit F.)

4. On August 13, 2025, at 2:02 PM, Simpson responded (Exhibit B-5) acknowledging that Plaintiff's "concerns and allegations warrant further information gathering and review" and offering to "discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures" -- a term of art defined exclusively in 34 C.F.R. § 106.30 within the Title IX framework. (Exhibit B-5.)

5. Between 4:20 PM and 7:31 PM on August 13, Plaintiff sent three emails (Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C) -- refusing the proposed meeting, demanding written communications, restoring OCR to the CC line, and citing §§ 106.44, 106.30, and 106.71. None of these emails contained any new facts about the underlying complaint. (Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C.)

6. On August 14, 2025, at 9:42 AM -- nineteen hours after B-5 -- Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination, stating: "the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested." Simpson stated the conduct "does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31's definitions of prohibited conduct, even if proven to be true." (Exhibit B-6.)

7. No new factual information about the underlying complaint entered the record between B-5 and B-6. The only intervening events were Plaintiff's three emails consisting entirely of protected activity under § 106.71. (Exhibits B-5, B-5A, B-5B, B-5C, B-6.)

8. Between June 30, 2025 and August 14, 2025 -- a period of 45 days -- no supportive measures were offered or provided to Plaintiff through the Title IX process. Defendants' communications consisted exclusively of requests to schedule meetings, directives to "engage with" offices, and jurisdictional disclaimers. (Exhibits B-0 through B-6.)

9. Simpson did not offer Plaintiff appeal rights following the B-6 dismissal, as required by § 106.45(b)(8). Simpson did not send simultaneous written notice to the respondent, as required by § 106.45(b)(3)(iii). (Exhibit B-6.)

10. Simpson performed zero of the five mandatory obligations under § 106.44(a): she did not promptly contact Plaintiff to discuss supportive measures, did not consider Plaintiff's wishes, did not inform Plaintiff of the availability of supportive measures, and did not explain the process for filing a formal complaint. (Exhibits B-5, B-6.)

11. During the 45-day period, Plaintiff's bank account balance fell to $16.65 (July 25) and negative $2.55 (August 12), while he lacked permanent housing. (Bank records, July 25 and August 12, 2025.)

tasks.name: Draft Retaliation Claim (Count II)
tasks.description: Count II: Title IX retaliation. Tests exhibit selection for protected activity, adverse action, and causal connection — the densest exhibit-to-element mapping.
tasks.parties_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendant Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit is a private university receiving federal financial assistance, located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

Individual actors referenced in the record include:
- Dr. Adam Wasilko, Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students
- Alicia Simpson, Title IX Coordinator and Director of Sexual Misconduct Prevention and Response
- Dr. Anne Sawa, Executive Director of Student Conduct
- Dr. Daniel Selcer, Associate Professor and Chair, Department of Philosophy
- Dr. Kristine Blair, Dean, McAnulty College of Liberal Arts
tasks.defendant_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendant Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit is a private university receiving federal financial assistance, located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
tasks.required_elements: (1) Protected activity (reporting, opposing, or participating); (2) Adverse action that would deter a reasonable person; (3) Causal connection between protected activity and adverse action
tasks.sumf:
1. Plaintiff engaged in continuous protected activity from June 30, 2025 through the present, including: reporting sex-based harassment to Wasilko (June 30); formal Title IX escalation citing § 106.44(a) (July 24); filing a complaint with OCR (July 26); filing a federal lawsuit (September 9); filing a TRO motion (October 8); and testifying at the TRO hearing (October 15). (Exhibits B-0, B-0A, B-3A; ECF docket.)

2. On July 28, 2025 -- two days after Plaintiff's OCR filing -- Wasilko removed OCR from the CC line on his responsive email. (Exhibit B-3.)

3. Between July 28 and October 2025, four institutional actors (Wasilko, Simpson, Sawa, and others) systematically removed OCR from the CC line on eight or more occasions, each time after Plaintiff restored OCR. No federal authority or Duquesne policy authorizes removal of a federal enforcement agency from institutional correspondence. (Exhibits B-3 through B-7 series.)

4. On August 13, 2025, at 2:02 PM, Simpson acknowledged Title IX jurisdiction using § 106.30 term of art "supportive measures" (Exhibit B-5). Between 4:20 PM and 7:31 PM, Plaintiff sent three emails consisting entirely of protected activity and zero new facts about the underlying complaint (Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C). On August 14, at 9:42 AM, Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination on an identical factual record (Exhibit B-6). The gap between jurisdictional acknowledgment and reversal was nineteen hours. (Exhibits B-5, B-5A, B-5B, B-5C, B-6.)

5. On October 6, 2025, Faculty C authorized Zoom access for Plaintiff. On October 8, Plaintiff filed the TRO motion. On October 15, Plaintiff testified at the TRO hearing. On October 17 -- two days after the hearing -- Selcer manufactured Zoom revocation. (TRO transcript; ECF docket; Timeline DB.)

6. Selcer provided shifting justifications for the Zoom denial and ultimately told Colleague A he "can't explain why" Zoom was denied. No university or departmental policy constrains Zoom access. (Exhibit L; SJ 7.3.)

7. In September 2025, Blair threatened Plaintiff with academic failure if he did not attend Selcer's class in person during active federal litigation against Selcer. (SAC ¶¶ 66, 76, 336.)

8. On February 13, 2026, Selcer reassigned Plaintiff's TA position and fabricated "performance issues raised by Faculty B" (Exhibit X-8). Faculty B's own same-day email (Exhibit X-7) made no mention of performance issues and described the reassignment request as keeping a "door open" with "no performance complaint." Selcer's email mid-sentence recharacterized "tensions and confrontations" as "performance issues" and threatened Plaintiff's stipend through Spring 2029. (Exhibits X-7, X-8.)

9. Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff had no performance issues. (TRO Tr. at *:*.)

tasks.name: Draft ADA/Section 504 Claim (Count III)
tasks.description: Count III: failure to accommodate. Only 11 of 38 exhibits are relevant — hardest discrimination task. Tests whether model correctly excludes Title IX exhibits.
tasks.parties_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendant Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit is a private university receiving federal financial assistance, located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

Individual actors referenced in the record include:
- Dr. Adam Wasilko, Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students
- Alicia Simpson, Title IX Coordinator and Director of Sexual Misconduct Prevention and Response
- Dr. Anne Sawa, Executive Director of Student Conduct
- Dr. Daniel Selcer, Associate Professor and Chair, Department of Philosophy
- Dr. Kristine Blair, Dean, McAnulty College of Liberal Arts
tasks.defendant_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendant Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit is a private university receiving federal financial assistance, located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
tasks.required_elements: (1) Qualified individual with disability; (2) Notice of disability and need for accommodation; (3) Request for reasonable accommodation; (4) Denial of accommodation; (5) Resulting harm
tasks.sumf:
1. Plaintiff's treating physician provided documentation stating: "I am a physician treating David Mullins. He's informed me he does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus, and from what he's described to me, I believe it's reasonable for him to have this fear." (Exhibit F.)

2. Plaintiff was at all relevant times an enrolled graduate student in good academic standing with no disciplinary record. Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff had no performance issues. (TRO Tr. at *:*.)

3. Plaintiff requested Zoom access for class participation as a medically necessary accommodation. Zoom is already in active use in the same department by Faculty D for comparable graduate coursework. (Faculty D Syllabus (Exhibit N).)

4. No university policy or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access. The Faculty Handbook 2025-2026, pp. 10-11, governs faculty course delivery modality, not individual student attendance accommodations. (Faculty Handbook; SJ 7.3.)

5. No interactive process was conducted. Defendants did not assess Plaintiff's specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services. (SJ 7.2.)

6. On August 25, 2025, Selcer imposed a three-week countdown on Plaintiff's Zoom access, after which Plaintiff would be required to attend in person regardless of medical documentation. This was a conditional, time-limited measure designed to expire and coerce meeting attendance. (Exhibit -- Selcer Aug 25 directive.)

7. Selcer testified under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference, citing "divides attention" and a post-COVID departmental finding, but could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial. (TRO Tr. at *:*.)

8. Colleague A, a student in Selcer's Graduate Teaching Seminar (Fall 2025), separately requested Zoom access. Selcer denied her request and told her he "can't explain why" Zoom was not available -- demonstrating a pattern of denial with no articulable basis. (Exhibit L.)

9. In A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools, 605 U.S. *** (2025), the Supreme Court held that student ADA and Section 504 claims use the same standard as claims in other settings, rejecting the heightened "bad faith or gross misjudgment" standard. Osseo expressly governs Section 504. (605 U.S. *** (2025).)

10. Duquesne University is a private university receiving federal financial assistance and is subject to Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act. (SAC ¶ 2.)

tasks.name: Oppose SJ on Fraud (Count IV)
tasks.description: Count IV: PA common law fraud — OPPOSITION BRIEF. Rebuts Block defense positions (Dkt. 34). Tests responsive advocacy under opposition standard.
tasks.parties_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendants:
- Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit, a private university receiving federal financial assistance
- Dr. Daniel Selcer, Associate Professor and Chair, Department of Philosophy
- Dr. Kristine Blair, Dean, McAnulty College of Liberal Arts
- Dr. Adam Wasilko, Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students
- Alicia Simpson, Title IX Coordinator and Director of Sexual Misconduct Prevention and Response
- Dr. Anne Sawa, Executive Director of Student Conduct
tasks.defendant_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendants:
- Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit, a private university receiving federal financial assistance
- Dr. Daniel Selcer, Associate Professor and Chair, Department of Philosophy
- Dr. Kristine Blair, Dean, McAnulty College of Liberal Arts
- Dr. Adam Wasilko, Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students
- Alicia Simpson, Title IX Coordinator and Director of Sexual Misconduct Prevention and Response
- Dr. Anne Sawa, Executive Director of Student Conduct
tasks.required_elements: (1) False representation of material fact; (2) Scienter (knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard); (3) Intent to induce reliance; (4) Justifiable reliance; (5) Resulting damages — Plaintiff must show genuine disputes of material fact on each element to defeat summary judgment
tasks.sumf:
1. Between June 30 and August 14, 2025, four defendants (Simpson, Wasilko, Sawa, and Selcer) made 36 nearly identical false statements fabricating a meeting requirement as a condition of receiving Title IX supportive measures. No provision of 34 C.F.R. Part 106 or TAP 61 conditions supportive measures on meeting attendance. (Exhibits B-0 through B-7; 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a); TAP 61.)

2. On July 25, 2025, Wasilko stated: "We cannot assist you or provide you with further guidance unless you are willing to engage with the appropriate offices." This conditioned all university assistance on a meeting/engagement requirement that appears nowhere in federal regulation or institutional policy. (Exhibit B-2.)

3. On July 24, 2025, Simpson's initial outreach (Exhibit B-1) did not offer any supportive measures. Simpson requested a meeting to "gather more information and discuss options" -- framing institutional obligations as discretionary. (Exhibit B-1.)

4. On August 14, 2025, Simpson stated the conduct "does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31's definitions of prohibited conduct, even if proven to be true" -- a determination that was wrong as a matter of law, because the reported conduct meets the stalking definition under both § 106.30 and TAP 61 Category 6. (Exhibit B-6; 34 C.F.R. § 106.30.)

5. Simpson testified under oath at the TRO hearing that she made her jurisdictional determination "entirely on the written record" with "no meeting" prior to decision, and that she "could have emailed" Plaintiff for clarification but "chose not to." This contradicts the position taken in B-1 through B-5 that meetings were necessary. (TRO Tr. at *:*.)

6. Four defendants systematically removed OCR from correspondence at critical junctures -- eight or more instances across four actors -- after Plaintiff repeatedly restored OCR to the CC line. This concealed the false meeting-requirement narrative from federal oversight. (Exhibits B-3 through B-7 series.)

7. On August 25, 2025, Selcer issued a falsified meeting summary misrepresenting what was discussed and agreed to, and imposed the three-week Zoom countdown without medical or policy basis. (Exhibit -- Selcer Aug 25 email.)

8. On October 22, 2025, Blair responded to Plaintiff's conflict-of-interest disclosure by stating: "I did need to consult Dr. Selcer as instructor... Both Dr. Selcer and I strongly concur that it is appropriate for you to remain enrolled in your required teaching seminar." Blair consulted the conflicted party about his own conflict -- seven days after that party was cross-examined under oath. (Exhibit K-2.)

9. Blair's TAP 33 bypass occurred without the independent review required by TAP 33 § IV.4. Plaintiff corrected Blair within 90 minutes with specific policy citations. Blair responded "no change." (Exhibits K-2, K-5.)

10. Senior university leadership received Plaintiff's escalations but did not intervene, relying on the false narrative maintained by the four defendants. (SAC ¶¶ 168-172; Exhibits B-0 through B-6.)

DAVID MULLINS, Plaintiff,
v.

PLAINTIFF'S MEMORANDUM IN OPPOSITION TO
DEFENDANTS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT ON COUNT IV — FRAUD
(Pennsylvania Common Law)

Civil Action No. 2:25-cv-01366-MJH

[PARTIES]

I. INTRODUCTION

Defendants move for summary judgment on Count IV, arguing Plaintiff
cannot establish the elements of Pennsylvania common law fraud. For the
reasons set forth below, genuine disputes of material fact preclude
summary judgment on each element.

II. DEFENDANTS' ARGUMENTS

[DEFENSE POSITIONS]

III. LEGAL STANDARD

Summary judgment is appropriate only where there is no genuine dispute
as to any material fact. Fed. R. Civ. P. 56(a). The Court must view all
facts and draw all reasonable inferences in the light most favorable to
the nonmovant. Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242, 255
(1986).

IV. PLAINTIFF'S COUNTER-STATEMENT OF MATERIAL FACTS IN DISPUTE

[SUMF]

--- VERIFIED CASE LAW (cite only these authorities) ---
[CORPUS]

--- DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE (38 exhibits from Plaintiff's case file) ---
Not all exhibits are relevant to this count. Cite only exhibits that
create genuine disputes of material fact. For each exhibit cited, state
which element it proves and why the evidence creates a triable issue.
[EXHIBIT POOL]

To defeat summary judgment, Plaintiff must show genuine disputes on:
[REQUIRED ELEMENTS]

tasks.name: Oppose SJ on Conspiracy (Count V)
tasks.description: Count V: PA civil conspiracy — OPPOSITION BRIEF. Rebuts Block defense positions (Dkts. 25 & 34). Tests whether model can flip CC-chain evidence against 'routine administration' defense.
tasks.parties_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendants:
- Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit, a private university receiving federal financial assistance
- Dr. Daniel Selcer, Associate Professor and Chair, Department of Philosophy
- Dr. Kristine Blair, Dean, McAnulty College of Liberal Arts
- Dr. Adam Wasilko, Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students
- Alicia Simpson, Title IX Coordinator and Director of Sexual Misconduct Prevention and Response
- Dr. Anne Sawa, Executive Director of Student Conduct
tasks.defendant_context:
PARTIES:

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University.

Defendants:
- Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit, a private university receiving federal financial assistance
- Dr. Daniel Selcer, Associate Professor and Chair, Department of Philosophy
- Dr. Kristine Blair, Dean, McAnulty College of Liberal Arts
- Dr. Adam Wasilko, Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students
- Alicia Simpson, Title IX Coordinator and Director of Sexual Misconduct Prevention and Response
- Dr. Anne Sawa, Executive Director of Student Conduct
tasks.required_elements: (1) Agreement between two or more actors; (2) Unlawful act or unlawful means; (3) Overt acts in furtherance; (4) Malice (legal or actual); (5) Resulting harm — Plaintiff must show genuine disputes of material fact on each element to defeat summary judgment
tasks.sumf:
1. Under Pennsylvania law, once a defendant joins a conspiracy, that defendant is jointly and severally liable for all tortious acts committed by any co-conspirator in furtherance of the conspiracy. Thompson Coal Co. v. Pike Coal Co., 488 Pa. 198, 412 A.2d 466, 472-73 (1979).

2. On October 22, 2025, Blair wrote: "I did need to consult Dr. Selcer as instructor to secure some additional details. Both Dr. Selcer and I strongly concur that it is appropriate for you to remain enrolled in your required teaching seminar." This is a written admission of joint determination with the conflicted party -- "both of us, together" rather than independent review. (Exhibit K-2.)

3. Blair consulted Selcer about a conflict-of-interest disclosure concerning Selcer himself -- seven days after Plaintiff cross-examined Selcer under oath at the TRO hearing on October 15, 2025. (Exhibit K-2; TRO Tr.)

4. TAP 33 § IV.4 requires independent review of conflict-of-interest disclosures. Blair's "consult[ation]" with the conflicted party does not constitute independent review under any reading of the policy. (TAP 33 § IV.4; Exhibit K-2.)

5. On February 13, 2026, Selcer's reassignment email referenced a "recommendation to the Dean of Liberal Arts" -- Blair's exact title -- demonstrating communication between Selcer and Blair in the days preceding the fabricated "performance issues" threat. (Exhibit X-8.)

6. The "recommendation to the Dean" language did not appear in the February 10, 2026 meeting between Selcer and Plaintiff, proving intervening communication between Selcer and Blair. (Exhibit X-6; Exhibit X-8.)

7. Four defendants systematically removed OCR and senior administrative oversight from correspondence at critical junctures, preventing reliance victims from receiving corrective information. Selcer removed Blair herself from the September 3 "final directive." (Exhibits B-3 through B-7; Selcer Sept 3 email.)

8. Plaintiff sent multiple escalations directly to Blair citing federal law, identifying specific violations, and requesting intervention. Blair did not intervene. Blair's silence, combined with her active coordination with Selcer, furthered the conspiracy's objective of maintaining defendants' control without independent oversight. (Exhibits K-2, K-5; SAC ¶¶ 326, 338, 417.)

9. The underlying torts committed by co-conspirators include: 36 false statements fabricating a meeting requirement (Conspiracy 1), systematic OCR removal across four actors (Conspiracy 2), and Selcer's fabrication of "performance issues" contradicted by the source he cited on the same day (Exhibits X-7, X-8). Blair need not have personally committed these acts to be liable for them under Thompson Coal. (412 A.2d at 472-73.)
tasks.base_prompt:
IN THE UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT
FOR THE WESTERN DISTRICT OF PENNSYLVANIA

CASE NO. 2:25-cv-01366-MJH

DAVID MULLINS, Plaintiff,
v.
DUQUESNE UNIVERSITY OF THE HOLY SPIRIT, et al., Defendants.

PLAINTIFF'S MEMORANDUM IN OPPOSITION TO
DEFENDANTS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT ON COUNT V — CIVIL CONSPIRACY
(Pennsylvania Common Law; 42 U.S.C. § 1985)

Civil Action No. 2:25-cv-01366-MJH

[PARTIES]

I. INTRODUCTION

Defendants move for summary judgment on Count V, arguing Plaintiff
cannot establish the elements of Pennsylvania civil conspiracy. For the
reasons set forth below, genuine disputes of material fact — including
direct evidence of coordinated action — preclude summary judgment.

II. DEFENDANTS' ARGUMENTS

[DEFENSE POSITIONS]

III. LEGAL STANDARD

Summary judgment is appropriate only where there is no genuine dispute
as to any material fact. Fed. R. Civ. P. 56(a). The Court must view all
facts and draw all reasonable inferences in the light most favorable to
the nonmovant. Circumstantial evidence of conspiracy is sufficient to
defeat summary judgment where it permits a reasonable inference of
agreement.

IV. PLAINTIFF'S COUNTER-STATEMENT OF MATERIAL FACTS IN DISPUTE

[SUMF]

--- VERIFIED CASE LAW (cite only these authorities) ---
[CORPUS]

--- DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE (38 exhibits from Plaintiff's case file) ---
Not all exhibits are relevant to this count. Cite only exhibits that
create genuine disputes of material fact. For each exhibit cited, state
which element it proves and why the evidence creates a triable issue.
[EXHIBIT POOL]

--- REQUIRED LEGAL ELEMENTS ---
To defeat summary judgment, Plaintiff must show genuine disputes on:
[REQUIRED ELEMENTS]

V. ARGUMENT
